content.text: An output metric measures what the Claude integration produces. An outcome metric measures what changed as a result. The distinction matters because outputs can be high while outcomes are zero — a system that processes 10,000 queries per day is generating output; whether that output saves time, reduces errors, or improves decisions is the outcome.

Enterprise Claude programmes fail their business case reviews not because they lack data, but because the data they collect is at the wrong level. Usage dashboards showing query volumes, session counts, and uptime are activity and output metrics. The steering committee wants to know: what business result has changed? Without outcome data, the programme cannot answer that question and cannot defend continued investment against competing priorities.

attrs.alt: Metric hierarchy showing activity metrics at the base, output metrics in the middle, and outcome metrics at the top

content.text: Every Claude integration generates metrics at all three levels of the hierarchy. Activity metrics are collected automatically by infrastructure. Output metrics require instrumentation. Outcome metrics require measurement design — often before the integration goes live, because the baseline (the pre-Claude state) must be captured before Claude changes it.
API calls per day, tokens consumed, uptime %, errors per hour
Query completion rate, user adoption rate (% of team using weekly), satisfaction score (CSAT)
Time per policy query (before: 8 min, after: 45 sec), HR helpdesk ticket volume (before: 400/month, after: 280/month), policy compliance error rate

Outcome metrics cannot be defined retrospectively. A programme that goes live without defining its outcome metrics will scramble to find evidence of impact when the steering committee asks at the 90-day review. The baseline — the pre-Claude state — must be captured before the integration changes the behaviour it is meant to change.
The outcome metric definition process happens in discovery, as part of the success criteria workshop:
Define the intended outcome in plain language:
'Reduce the time HR staff spend answering routine policy questions.' Not 'increase usage of the Claude system.'
'Time per policy query (measured via time-motion study or help-desk ticket data)' or 'Volume of routine HR helpdesk tickets per month.'
Measure the proxy before deployment. If the baseline data does not exist, create a measurement instrument now — a simple log, a timer in the existing workflow, a ticket category tag.
Agree the minimum outcome improvement that would justify the investment. '20% reduction in time per policy query within 90 days of full deployment.'
Define the measurement method for post-deployment:
How will the same metric be measured after Claude is live? The post-deployment measurement method must be equivalent to the baseline measurement, or the comparison is invalid.
Outcome Metric Patterns for Common Claude Use Cases
Time to answer a policy/knowledge question
Volume of questions escalated to human experts
Helpdesk ticket log or time-motion study
Time to produce a first draft
Review and revision cycles per document
Project management tool time logs or manual log
Average handling time per support case
Time to complete a contract review
Proportion of contracts returned for re-review after signing
Time from data request to insight delivered
Analyst requests requiring data re-pull due to errors
Analyst time tracking or Jira/project tool
Time from PR submission to review completion
Post-merge defect rate (CI failures or bug reports)
Git commit history and issue tracker

attrs.alt: Goodhart's Law examples: when a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure — showing gaming behaviours and better alternatives

content.text: Goodhart's Law — 'When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure' — applies forcefully to Claude adoption metrics. A programme that reports query volume as its primary success metric creates incentives for the team and BU to inflate query volume without improving outcomes. A programme that reports adoption rate creates incentives to pressure users into logging in without using the tool.
The defence against Goodhart's Law is metric pairing: every primary metric is paired with a complementary metric that cannot be gamed in the same direction. Pairing rules:
Queries processed (volume) pairs with task completion rate (quality). Inflating queries produces no benefit unless tasks are completed.
Login rate (adoption) pairs with tasks completed per active user per week (depth). Logging in without using adds logins but depresses depth.
CSAT score (satisfaction) pairs with return usage rate (behaviour). Users who are satisfied return; a high CSAT with falling return usage signals something is wrong.

Outcome metrics often take time to manifest. A contract review assistant may reduce contract processing time starting from week 2 of deployment — but the outcome metric (contract throughput per FTE) may take 90 days to show statistical significance. Programmes need both:
(early signals that outcomes are on track): adoption rate of active users, task completion rate in the first 2 weeks, initial time-saving measurements on sampled tasks.
(the outcome metric itself): average time per task over 90 days, error rate reduction over 6 months, FTE required per unit of work over a quarter.

A reporting framework that tracks both gives the steering committee confidence that outcomes are developing even before the lagging metric has stabilised. It also provides an early warning signal if the leading indicators suggest the outcome will not be reached — allowing corrective action before the 90-day review.

The output metric trap is the most common failure mode in Claude programme measurement: the programme team works hard on outputs (queries processed, satisfaction scores, uptime percentage) because these are easy to collect and show progress, while the outcome metrics remain unmeasured or poorly defined. The result is a programme that can demonstrate it is working but cannot demonstrate it is achieving anything.

If your quarterly business review slide deck shows query volume, system uptime, and a satisfaction score but no time-saving data, no error rate reduction, and no cost comparison — you have fallen into the output metric trap. The steering committee will notice.

Escaping the output metric trap requires investing in outcome measurement infrastructure before go-live: time-tracking instruments, baseline data collection, ticket categorisation changes, or process timing studies. This investment is typically 2–4 days of analyst time and produces the measurement foundation the programme needs to defend its business case at every subsequent review.

Outcome metrics should be directly linked to the programme's Objectives and Key Results (OKRs). The linkage makes explicit which metric proves which objective, and prevents the common failure of OKRs that cannot be measured with the data the programme actually collects.

# OKR to metric linkage — structured as a data object for programmatic reporting

programme_okrs = [
    {
        "objective": "Reduce HR operations overhead through intelligent self-service",
        "key_results": [
            {
                "kr": "Reduce average time to answer a routine HR policy query from 8 minutes to under 2 minutes",
                "metric": "avg_policy_query_time_minutes",
                "baseline": 8.0,
                "target": 2.0,
                "current": None,  # populated from measurement pipeline
                "measurement_method": "Time-motion study: 50 sampled queries per month, timed by analyst",
                "measurement_frequency": "monthly",
            },
            {
                "kr": "Reduce HR helpdesk ticket volume for routine policy questions by 30% within 90 days",
                "metric": "monthly_routine_policy_tickets",
                "baseline": 420,  # avg tickets/month in 6 months pre-deployment
                "target": 294,    # 420 * 0.70
                "current": None,
                "measurement_method": "Helpdesk ticket tagging: category 'routine-policy' auto-tagged since 2025-01",
                "measurement_frequency": "monthly",
            },
        ],
        "paired_health_metrics": [
            "task_completion_rate",       # quality paired with volume
            "escalation_to_human_rate",   # must not rise as ticket volume falls
        ],
    },
]

def okr_progress(okr_data: dict) -> list[dict]:
    results = []
    for kr in okr_data["key_results"]:
        if kr["current"] is None:
            status = "not_yet_measured"
            progress_pct = None
        else:
            total_change = kr["baseline"] - kr["target"]
            actual_change = kr["baseline"] - kr["current"]
            # For metrics where lower is better (time, tickets)
            progress_pct = round(actual_change / total_change * 100, 1) if total_change != 0 else 0
            status = "on_track" if progress_pct >= 50 else "at_risk"
        results.append({
            "key_result": kr["kr"][:60] + "...",
            "status": status,
            "progress": f"{progress_pct}%" if progress_pct is not None else "pending",
        })
    return results

for item in okr_progress(programme_okrs[0]):
    print(f"[{item['status'].upper()}] {item['key_result']}")
    print(f"  Progress: {item['progress']}")

# [NOT_YET_MEASURED] Reduce average time to answer a routine HR policy query...
#   Progress: pending
# [NOT_YET_MEASURED] Reduce HR helpdesk ticket volume for routine policy que...
#   Progress: pending

Different metrics are reported at different cadences to different audiences. Mixing all metrics into a single report for all audiences produces both information overload for executives and insufficient detail for the programme team.
Activity metrics: API health, query volume, error rates, latency
Output metrics: adoption rate, task completion rate, issues identified this week
Output + outcome: adoption vs. target, early outcome indicators, open issues affecting BU
Programme Director, CFO, CTO, BU sponsors
Outcome metrics vs. business case: ROI progress, OKR status, strategic risks
Aggregated outcomes: FTE hours saved, cost reduction achieved, strategic alignment

An outcome metric without a baseline is meaningless — it cannot tell you whether the programme improved anything. If a contract review assistant processes 200 contracts per FTE per month and the pre-integration rate was 180 contracts per FTE per month, the programme achieved an 11% improvement. Without the baseline, the 200 figure is just a number with no interpretation.

Baselines must be established before go-live, not retrospectively. Collecting baseline data after the integration is live introduces two problems: the measurement process itself may change behaviour (Hawthorne effect), and the pre-integration period is no longer cleanly observable. In practice, a 4–8 week pre-go-live period of structured baseline data collection is standard for any integration with time-saving or error-reduction outcome metrics.
What to Measure in the Baseline Period

Time from task initiation to completion for the specific tasks the integration will assist with. Measure a representative sample — 20–50 instances per task type — to get a stable mean and distribution.
Frequency of downstream corrections, rework cycles, or escalations attributable to the task. This is the denominator for the error rate reduction outcome metric.
How many tasks of each type are completed per person per period (day/week/month). This is the baseline for throughput improvement metrics.
Fully-loaded cost of completing the task in the pre-integration state. Multiply time by FTE cost rate, add any external costs (legal review fees, external vendor calls, etc.).
User satisfaction (CSAT) for the current process:
A short survey before integration gives the comparison point for post-integration satisfaction. Without this, any post-integration score floats against an unknown reference.

If you cannot collect a baseline before go-live, collect it in the first 2 weeks of deployment using a control group: a team that uses the old process while the pilot group uses the integration. Control groups are more expensive to run but produce valid comparison data when pre-go-live measurement is not possible.

Every Claude integration should define its outcome metrics using this structured process during the discovery phase. The process prevents vague metric definitions, ensures measurability, and produces the baseline and acceptance criteria the programme needs before go-live.
State the business problem in one sentence:
'Our contract review team takes an average of 4 hours per contract and has a backlog of 200 contracts at any given time.' If you cannot state the problem in one sentence, the scope is too broad.
Identify the direct process outcome that would indicate the problem is solved:
'Contract review time reduces to under 2 hours and the backlog clears.' This is the outcome, not the output.
Define the metric that measures the outcome, including unit and measurement method:
'Average time from contract receipt to approval decision, measured in hours, calculated from timestamps in the contract management system.'
Specify the target and the baseline method:
'Target: <= 2.5 hours (37.5% reduction from baseline). Baseline: 4-week average from the contract management system before integration go-live.'
'Measured weekly from the contract management system by the operations analyst. Reported in the programme steering committee monthly review. Alert if 4-week rolling average exceeds 3 hours.'

This five-step definition process produces a single outcome metric card. A typical Claude integration should have 2–4 outcome metric cards — one per primary business objective. More than 4 primary outcome metrics usually indicates scope creep or insufficient prioritisation.
Common Outcome Metrics by Integration Type

While every integration's outcome metrics should be derived from its specific business problem, the following patterns recur across Claude integration types and provide a useful starting reference during discovery:
Average handle time reduction (minutes per ticket); first contact resolution rate
Time to answer (from question to confident response); escalation rate to specialists
Ticket/chat logs; time-tracking; specialist escalation queue volume
Review time per document; rework cycle rate
Document management system timestamps; rework tracking
Time from ticket to merge; bug escape rate (defects in production)
Version control/CI timestamps; post-release defect tracking
Time from data availability to report delivery; revision cycles
Analyst time logs; report revision tracking
Review cycle time; external legal spend per contract
Analysis turnaround time; analyst queries per week capacity
Project management system; analyst time logs

These are starting points, not prescriptions. The specific target value (e.g., 30% AHT reduction vs 15% AHT reduction) depends on the baseline measurement and the business case established during discovery. Use these patterns to prompt stakeholder discussions about what 'good' looks like for their specific context.

Outcome metrics measured but never reported are not outcome metrics — they are data collection exercises. The reporting cadence must be defined in the success criteria specification and agreed with stakeholders before go-live.
Leading indicators (adoption, completion rate, error flags)
Early warning; course correction before monthly review
All primary outcome metrics vs. baseline and target
Track progress; escalate risks; adjust scope
Full outcome metric suite; ROI calculation; NFR compliance
Justify continued investment; update targets if business context changed
Aggregate ROI; strategic alignment; next-phase recommendations
Investment renewal decision; programme expansion scope

The most common reporting failure is reporting output metrics to the steering committee and outcome metrics only internally. Outcome metrics are precisely what the steering committee needs to make investment decisions — if they are seeing query volumes and uptime percentages, they are being managed on the wrong data.

Dashboard design should separate leading indicators (available weekly) from outcome metrics (stabilised over 4-8 weeks). A dashboard that mixes 7-day query volume alongside 90-day throughput improvement is visually confusing and easy to misread. Separate panels with clear time windows prevent misinterpretation.
When Outcome Metrics Are Difficult to Isolate

Outcome metrics assume a relatively clean causal relationship: before Claude, the task took X; after Claude, the task takes Y; the difference is Claude's contribution. In practice, other changes often occur simultaneously — a new team member, a process improvement, a system upgrade — making it difficult to isolate Claude's contribution.
Three approaches manage this attribution problem:

Run a parallel cohort of users on the old process while the pilot cohort uses Claude. Both groups are measured on the same metrics over the same period. The difference between the groups is Claude's contribution, controlling for external changes that affect both groups equally.

If a control group is not feasible, compare the rate of change in the outcome metric before and after go-live. External changes affect the metric level; Claude affects the slope. A before-after slope change is attributable to Claude if no other major change occurred in that period.

State explicitly what proportion of the measured improvement is attributed to Claude vs. other factors, and document the assumptions behind the split. This is less rigorous than a control group but maintains transparency with stakeholders about the attribution uncertainty.

# Attribution model: conservative Claude contribution estimate
attribution_report = {
    "metric": "average_review_time_hours",
    "baseline": 4.0,
    "post_integration_90day_avg": 2.4,
    "raw_improvement": 1.6,  # hours reduction
    "concurrent_changes": [
        {
            "change": "New contract management system UI",
            "estimated_contribution": 0.2,
            "source": "PM estimate from UI change impact analysis"
        }
    ],
    "claude_attributed_improvement": 1.4,  # 1.6 - 0.2
    "claude_attribution_confidence": "medium",
    "attribution_note": (
        "0.2h improvement attributed to CMS UI upgrade based on PM estimate. "
        "Attribution is approximate — a control group was not feasible. "
        "Conservative estimate: Claude accounts for at least 75% of the measured improvement."
    )
}

Each outcome metric should be fully specified in a structured card before the success criteria document is finalised. A partial specification — 'we will measure customer satisfaction' — is not a metric; it is a category. The following template produces a complete, unambiguous metric specification that can be operationalised without further discussion:

OUTCOME METRIC SPECIFICATION CARD

Metric name:       [Short name used in dashboards and reports]
Business objective: [Which objective this metric evidences progress on]
Problem statement:  [One sentence: what business problem does this measure]
Definition:         [Precise operational definition: what is counted/measured,
                     over what time window, for which user population]
Unit of measurement: [Hours / percentage / count / ratio / currency]
Data source:        [System and specific field or event that produces the raw data]
Calculation:        [Formula or algorithm for computing the metric from raw data]
Baseline:           [Value and collection method before go-live]
Target:             [Value and date by which it must be reached]
Alert threshold:    [Value that triggers an escalation review]
Reporting cadence:  [Weekly pulse / monthly review / quarterly retrospective]
Metric owner:       [Named individual responsible for data collection and reporting]
Review owner:       [Named individual responsible for interpreting results and
                     recommending action]
Notes / assumptions: [Attribution assumptions, known data quality limitations,
                      or seasonal factors that affect interpretation]
Distinguishing Outcome Metrics from Proxy Metrics

A proxy metric is a measurable quantity that is believed to correlate with a business outcome but is not the outcome itself. Proxy metrics are useful when the true outcome is too slow to observe, too expensive to measure directly, or too noisy to track reliably. But treating a proxy metric as if it were an outcome metric is a category error with real consequences.

Example: Suppose the business outcome is 'reduce legal risk exposure.' The true outcome metric would be the frequency or cost of legal disputes — but legal disputes may be infrequent and slow to manifest. A proxy metric is 'percentage of contracts reviewed before signature' — a measurable quantity that is believed to correlate with legal risk reduction. If the proxy rises from 60% to 95%, stakeholders may feel that legal risk is being managed — but the proxy can rise even if the quality of contract review falls. A contract reviewed in 2 minutes by someone who just asks Claude for a boilerplate response has been 'reviewed' in the metric but not in the outcome sense.

Identifying proxies vs. outcomes requires asking: 'Is this what we actually care about, or is it something we believe leads to what we care about?' If the answer is 'leads to,' the metric is a proxy. Proxies should be labelled as such in the success criteria specification, with an explicit statement of the assumed causal mechanism and how it will be validated.
Coverage rises but review quality falls; risk unchanged
Annual legal incident review comparing pre/post integration periods
Employees satisfied with the tool but not using it for high-value tasks
Compare task completion rates and output quality, not just satisfaction scores
Volume rises due to casual curiosity, not substituting specialist calls
Track specialist escalation ticket volume separately from query volume
Developers accept poor suggestions to improve the metric
Measure time-to-merge and defect rate alongside acceptance rate

The safest proxy metrics are those where gaming the metric in the favourable direction requires actually achieving the outcome. A proxy that can be gamed in the wrong direction — where the metric looks good while the outcome deteriorates — is a liability, not an asset.

Outcome metrics only work when business stakeholders have actively agreed to them — not just been informed of them. A metrics framework developed entirely by the programme team and presented to the steering committee as a fait accompli is likely to be contested when results are unfavourable. Contested metrics lead to steering committee energy spent on methodology debates rather than programme decisions.
Three practices ensure genuine stakeholder agreement before go-live:

A 2-hour structured session with the BU lead, operations lead, and finance representative to define the 2-4 primary outcome metrics. The workshop produces signed metric definition cards, not just slides.

Ask the steering committee before go-live: 'If we report this metric in 6 months and it looks good, what would make you distrust the result? What would you want to check?' The answers reveal hidden assumptions about the metric that, if unstated, would become disputes after the fact.

Document the threshold at which the programme will be declared successful, failed, or requiring course correction. 'The programme is successful if the 90-day average contract review time is below 2.5 hours. It requires course correction if it is between 2.5 and 3.5 hours. A 90-day average above 3.5 hours triggers a programme review.' Written thresholds eliminate post-hoc interpretation arguments.

Activity metrics (queries processed, uptime) are easy to collect but do not prove business value. Output metrics (adoption rate, task completion rate, satisfaction score) demonstrate the integration is being used and working, but are not sufficient to defend investment. Outcome metrics — measurable changes in business results — are the only metrics that answer the steering committee's question: what changed because of this programme?

Outcome metrics must be defined in discovery, before deployment, with baselines captured before Claude changes the behaviour being measured. Each metric should be paired with a complementary metric to prevent Goodhart's Law gaming. Leading indicators provide early signals that outcomes are on track; lagging indicators confirm the outcome once it has stabilised. The metric framework should be directly linked to the programme's OKRs so that every quarterly business review can show unambiguous progress (or lack of progress) against the committed business case.
